title: Quiz: Quantum Measurement
description: Test your understanding of quantum measurement, the Born rule, and wave function collapse

questions.question: For a qubit in state |ψ⟩ = (3/5)|0⟩ + (4/5)|1⟩, what is the probability of measuring |0⟩?
questions.explanation: By the Born rule, P(|0⟩) = |α|² = |3/5|² = 9/25 = 0.36. The probability is the squared magnitude of the amplitude, not the amplitude itself.

questions.question: After measuring a qubit in state |ψ⟩ = α|0⟩ + β|1⟩ and obtaining outcome |0⟩, what is the post-measurement state?
questions.explanation: Upon measurement, wave function collapse occurs. If we measure |0⟩, the state collapses to |0⟩ regardless of the original amplitudes. This is a normalized state with amplitude 1 for |0⟩.

questions.question: If you measure the same qubit twice in the computational basis without any operations in between, you will always get the same result.
questions.explanation: True! After the first measurement, the state collapses to an eigenstate (|0⟩ or |1⟩). Subsequent measurements in the same basis will yield the same result with 100% probability.

questions.question: What is the state |−⟩ in the Hadamard basis expressed in the computational basis?
questions.explanation: The Hadamard basis states are |+⟩ = (|0⟩ + |1⟩)/√2 and |−⟩ = (|0⟩ - |1⟩)/√2. The minus state has a relative phase of -1 between the computational basis states.

questions.question: If you measure |0⟩ in the Hadamard basis {|+⟩, |−⟩}, what is P(|+⟩)?
questions.explanation: |0⟩ = (|+⟩ + |−⟩)/√2, so |0⟩ is an equal superposition of |+⟩ and |−⟩. By the Born rule, P(|+⟩) = |⟨+|0⟩|² = |1/√2|² = 1/2.

questions.question: Which property must projection operators satisfy?
questions.explanation: Projection operators are idempotent, meaning P² = P. Applying a projector twice is the same as applying it once - once you've projected onto a subspace, projecting again changes nothing.

questions.question: In quantum mechanics, measurement reveals a pre-existing value that was simply unknown before the measurement.
questions.explanation: False! This is a crucial distinction. Quantum measurement doesn't reveal a hidden value - the superposition IS the complete reality. Measurement creates a definite outcome from genuine quantum indefiniteness.

questions.question: Which of the following are true about quantum measurement? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Measurement in quantum mechanics is reversible
We can measure in any orthonormal basis, not just computational

questions.explanation: a) Correct: The Born rule states P = |amplitude|². b) Incorrect: Measurement is fundamentally irreversible; we cannot recover the original superposition. c) Correct: According to standard quantum mechanics, collapse is instantaneous. d) Correct: Any orthonormal basis can be used for measurement.
